name: Population Model
description: A world-state model that tracks groups of agents, citizens, creatures, or factions through spawning, migration, growth, attrition, demand, and capacity rules.
design_notes: Population models become useful when players can see how space, resources, safety, demand, and policies change group behavior. The system should define update order, spawn budgets, capacity limits, migration rules, save snapshots, and debug views before it drives economy or ecology.
design_purpose: Create living-world behavior from group-level rules rather than individual scripting.
Connect resources, buildings, hazards, territory, weather, and player policy to population outcomes.
Give designers scalable control over crowds, colonies, wildlife, or city residents.
player_fantasy: The player feels the world responds to pressure as populations grow, move, decline, or recover over time.

parameters.name: simulation_id
parameters.description: Simulation instance owning population groups and update policy.

parameters.name: population_group_id
parameters.description: Group whose count, needs, or behavior is modeled.

parameters.name: region_id
parameters.description: Spatial or logical area containing the population group.

parameters.name: spawn_budget
parameters.description: Cap used to create representative entities from group state.

parameters.name: despawn_policy_id
parameters.description: Rule for folding visible agents back into aggregate state.

parameters.name: demand_curve_id
parameters.description: Curve that converts conditions into growth or migration pressure.

parameters.name: capacity_rule_id
parameters.description: Rule that limits sustainable population in a region.

parameters.name: deterministic_update_order
parameters.description: Ordered passes used so runs and saves update consistently.

edge_cases: A region loses capacity after a disaster and must decide which agents migrate or despawn.
Visible agents and aggregate population counts diverge after pooling or save/load.
Population growth runs offline and exceeds food, shelter, or performance budgets.
A faction is eliminated in one system but still has scheduled spawn events.
Migration and territory control update in different orders and produce contradictory ownership.
common_bugs: Spawned agents are counted again as population growth when folded back into aggregate state.
Despawn pooling removes quest-critical or player-targeted entities.
Population ticks use unordered region maps and produce non-reproducible results.
Capacity changes are not saved, so population rebounds after reload.
Debug displays show visible agents only and hide aggregate population pressure.
balancing_notes: Aggregate populations let designers simulate scale, but players need enough visible agents to believe the model.
Hard capacity caps are readable; soft caps can feel more organic but need explanation.
Growth and migration should react slowly enough for players to notice causes before outcomes.
accessibility_notes: Provide readable dashboards, map overlays, and alert thresholds for population changes.
Avoid relying on dense crowd visuals alone to communicate population state.
Allow pausing or slow simulation modes when many population alerts arrive together.

implementation_notes.unity: Keep aggregate population state in data containers and spawn visible agents through a pooling manager. Update regions in deterministic_update_order, then write save snapshots containing population_group_id counts, region capacity, and queued spawn events.
implementation_notes.godot: Use Resources for population groups and a simulation manager for region passes. Fold despawned agents back into population data explicitly, and provide debug overlays for region_id, capacity, demand, and spawn budget.
implementation_notes.unreal: Mass Entity or custom subsystems can represent large populations while Actors are only the visible subset. Server or simulation authority should own migration, spawning, and despawn policy to keep save and replay state consistent.
implementation_notes.web: Model populations as aggregate records and materialize visible agents only near the player or camera. Use sorted region ids and fixed update passes so browser refreshes and deterministic replays preserve population outcomes.

relationships.reason: Population models need demand, need, or group state to produce believable growth and migration.
Population supply and demand values can feed dynamic pricing rules.
Population spawns and combat spawn directors need shared budgets to avoid overcrowding or pacing spikes.

example_games.title: Cities: Skylines
example_games.note: Uses population and demand models to shape city growth.

example_games.title: Dwarf Fortress
example_games.note: Uses population, creatures, and settlement state in systemic simulation.
